name: Insertion Sort
definition: Insertion Sort builds a sorted array one element at a time by taking each element and inserting it into its correct position among the previously sorted elements. Like sorting a hand of playing cards.
intuition: Imagine sorting playing cards in your hand. You pick up one card at a time and insert it into the correct position among the cards you already hold.
steps: Start from the second element (index 1), treat first element as sorted
Compare key with each element to its left
Shift elements that are greater than key one position to the right
Insert key into the correct position
pseudocode:
INSERTION-SORT(A):
  for j = 2 to A.length
    key = A[j]
    i = j - 1
    while i > 0 and A[i] > key
      A[i+1] = A[i]
      i = i - 1
    A[i+1] = key
advantages: Efficient for small or nearly sorted arrays
Online — can sort as it receives data

comparisons.merge_sort: Insertion Sort is simpler and faster for small arrays but O(n²) vs Merge Sort's O(n log n) for large inputs.
comparisons.bubble_sort: Both are O(n²) but Insertion Sort performs fewer swaps and is faster in practice.
comparisons.selection_sort: Both O(n²) but Insertion Sort is stable and faster on nearly sorted data.

loop_invariant: At the start of each iteration of the for loop, the subarray A[1..j-1] consists of the elements originally in A[1..j-1] but in sorted order.

name: Merge Sort
definition: Merge Sort is a divide-and-conquer algorithm that recursively splits an array into two halves, sorts each half, and merges them back into a sorted array. It is defined by the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n).
intuition: Split a deck of cards into two piles. Sort each pile separately (recursively). Then merge by always picking the smaller top card from either pile.
steps: Divide: Split array A[p..r] at midpoint q = floor((p+r)/2)
Conquer: Recursively sort A[p..q] and A[q+1..r]
Combine: Merge the two sorted subarrays using MERGE procedure
MERGE compares elements from both halves and places smaller one into result
Base case: array of length 1 is already sorted
pseudocode:
MERGE-SORT(A, p, r):
  if p < r
    q = floor((p+r)/2)
    MERGE-SORT(A, p, q)
    MERGE-SORT(A, q+1, r)
    MERGE(A, p, q, r)

MERGE(A, p, q, r):
  n1 = q - p + 1
  n2 = r - q
  create arrays L[1..n1+1] and R[1..n2+1]
  for i=1 to n1: L[i] = A[p+i-1]
  for j=1 to n2: R[j] = A[q+j]
  L[n1+1] = R[n2+1] = infinity (sentinel)
  i=1, j=1
  for k=p to r:
    if L[i] <= R[j]: A[k]=L[i], i++
    else: A[k]=R[j], j++
disadvantages: Slower than Quick Sort in practice for small n

comparisons.quick_sort: Merge Sort is stable and always O(n log n) but uses O(n) space. Quick Sort is faster in practice but O(n²) worst case.
comparisons.heap_sort: Both O(n log n) worst case. Merge Sort is stable, Heap Sort is in-place.
comparisons.insertion_sort: Merge Sort is far better for large n. Use Insertion Sort for n < 20.

name: Quick Sort
definition: Quick Sort selects a pivot element, partitions the array so all elements smaller than pivot come before it and larger ones after, then recursively sorts both partitions. Average case T(n) = 2T(n/2) + Θ(n).
intuition: Choose any element as a pivot. After partitioning, that pivot is in its final sorted position. Recursively sort both halves around it.
steps: For j = p to r-1: if A[j] <= pivot, increment i and swap A[i] with A[j]
Place pivot at A[i+1] by swapping with A[r]
pseudocode:
QUICKSORT(A, p, r):
  if p < r
    q = PARTITION(A, p, r)
    QUICKSORT(A, p, q-1)
    QUICKSORT(A, q+1, r)

PARTITION(A, p, r):
  x = A[r]   // pivot
  i = p - 1
  for j = p to r-1
    if A[j] <= x
      i = i + 1
      swap A[i] with A[j]
  swap A[i+1] with A[r]
  return i+1

steps: Pivot 6 is now at correct position (index 3)

comparisons.merge_sort: Quick Sort uses O(log n) space vs O(n). Quick Sort faster in practice but not stable and has O(n²) worst case.
comparisons.heap_sort: Heap Sort guarantees O(n log n) worst case. Quick Sort is faster in practice but not stable.
comparisons.insertion_sort: Quick Sort dominates for large n. Insertion Sort wins for very small n (< 20 elements).

name: Heap Sort
definition: Heap Sort uses a max-heap data structure to sort an array. It first builds a max-heap from the input, then repeatedly extracts the maximum element and places it at the end of the array.
intuition: Build a max-heap (parent always >= children). The root is always the maximum. Swap root with last element, reduce heap size, fix heap, repeat.
steps: BUILD-MAX-HEAP: Convert array into a max-heap in O(n)
For i = n down to 2: swap A[1] with A[i]
Call MAX-HEAPIFY to restore max-heap property
pseudocode:
HEAPSORT(A):
  BUILD-MAX-HEAP(A)
  for i = A.length downto 2
    swap A[1] with A[i]
    A.heap-size = A.heap-size - 1
    MAX-HEAPIFY(A, 1)

MAX-HEAPIFY(A, i):
  l = LEFT(i), r = RIGHT(i)
  largest = i
  if l <= heap-size and A[l] > A[i]: largest = l
  if r <= heap-size and A[r] > A[largest]: largest = r
  if largest != i:
    swap A[i] with A[largest]
    MAX-HEAPIFY(A, largest)
disadvantages: Slower than Quick Sort in practice

name: Counting Sort
definition: Counting Sort is a non-comparison-based sorting algorithm that counts the occurrences of each element and uses these counts to determine sorted positions. It beats the Ω(n log n) lower bound by not using comparisons.
intuition: Count how many elements are ≤ each value. Then each element knows exactly where it belongs in the sorted output.
steps: Find range k (maximum value in array)
Initialize count array C[0..k] with zeros
Count occurrences: C[A[j]]++ for each element
Accumulate: C[i] = C[i] + C[i-1] (how many elements ≤ i)
Build output: place A[j] at position C[A[j]], decrement C[A[j]]
pseudocode:
COUNTING-SORT(A, B, k):
  let C[0..k] be new array, initialize to 0
  for j = 1 to A.length: C[A[j]] += 1
  for i = 1 to k: C[i] = C[i] + C[i-1]
  for j = A.length downto 1:
    B[C[A[j]]] = A[j]
    C[A[j]] -= 1
when_to_use: When k = O(n), i.e., range of values is proportional to number of elements
advantages: O(n+k) time — beats comparison lower bound
Used as subroutine in Radix Sort
disadvantages: Only works for integer keys in limited range
O(k) extra space — bad if k >> n

name: Binary Search
definition: Binary Search finds a target value in a sorted array by repeatedly halving the search space. Each comparison eliminates half the remaining candidates, giving O(log n) time.
intuition: Like finding a word in a dictionary — open to the middle, decide which half your word is in, repeat.
steps: If target < A[mid]: high = mid - 1 (search left half)
If target > A[mid]: low = mid + 1 (search right half)
Repeat until low > high (not found, return -1)
pseudocode:
BINARY-SEARCH(A, n, target):
  low = 0, high = n-1
  while low <= high
    mid = floor((low + high) / 2)
    if A[mid] == target: return mid
    elif A[mid] < target: low = mid + 1
    else: high = mid - 1
  return -1  // not found

steps: low=5, high=6 → mid=5, A[5]=23 == 23 → FOUND at index 5

comparisons.linear_search: Binary Search O(log n) vs Linear O(n). Binary requires sorted array.
comparisons.hash_table: Hash gives O(1) average lookup but Binary Search requires no extra space beyond sorted array.

name: Asymptotic Notation
definition: Asymptotic notation characterizes the running time of algorithms in terms of input size n, ignoring constants and lower-order terms. It describes worst-case (O), best-case (Ω), and tight (Θ) bounds.
intuition: We don't care if an algorithm takes 3n² + 5n + 2 steps — we just care that it grows like n². Constants depend on hardware; growth rate is what matters.

big_theta.meaning: Tight bound — sandwiched between c₁g(n) and c₂g(n) for all n ≥ n₀
big_theta.formal: Θ(g(n)) = {f(n): ∃ c₁,c₂,n₀>0 s.t. c₁g(n) ≤ f(n) ≤ c₂g(n) ∀ n≥n₀}

big_o.meaning: Upper bound — f(n) ≤ cg(n) for large n. Worst case.
big_o.formal: O(g(n)) = {f(n): ∃ c,n₀>0 s.t. f(n) ≤ cg(n) ∀ n≥n₀}

big_omega.meaning: Lower bound — f(n) ≥ cg(n) for large n. Best case.
big_omega.formal: Ω(g(n)) = {f(n): ∃ c,n₀>0 s.t. f(n) ≥ cg(n) ∀ n≥n₀}

little_o.meaning: Strictly less than — f grows strictly slower than g. Not tight.

little_omega.meaning: Strictly greater than — f grows strictly faster than g.

common_complexities.name: Constant
common_complexities.example: Array index access, hash table lookup

common_complexities.name: Linearithmic
common_complexities.example: Merge Sort, Heap Sort, Quick Sort average

common_complexities.name: Quadratic
common_complexities.example: Bubble Sort, Insertion Sort worst, nested loops

common_complexities.name: Exponential
common_complexities.example: Naive Fibonacci, Travelling Salesman brute force

key_theorems: If f(n) = Θ(g(n)), then f(n) = O(g(n)) and f(n) = Ω(g(n))
Transitivity: if f=O(g) and g=O(h) then f=O(h)
For polynomial p(n) = aₖnᵏ + ... + a₀ with aₖ > 0: p(n) = Θ(nᵏ)

name: Master Theorem
definition: The Master Theorem provides a direct solution to recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a≥1 and b>1, which arise from divide-and-conquer algorithms.
intuition: The recurrence compares the work done at the top level f(n) vs the total work at the leaves nˡᵒᵍᵦᵃ. Whoever is polynomially larger dominates the total.
formula: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n), where a ≥ 1, b > 1

cases.condition: f(n) = O(n^(log_b(a) - ε)) for some ε > 0

cases.meaning: Work is balanced at every level

cases.condition: f(n) = Ω(n^(log_b(a) + ε)) and af(n/b) ≤ cf(n)

steps: Identify a (number of subproblems), b (size reduction factor), f(n) (combine cost)

name: Dynamic Programming
definition: Dynamic Programming solves optimization problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, solving each subproblem exactly once, and storing results in a table (memoization/tabulation) to avoid redundant computation.
intuition: If computing F(5) requires F(4) and F(3), and F(4) also requires F(3) — why compute F(3) twice? Compute it once, store it, reuse it.

two_conditions.optimal_substructure: Optimal solution to the problem contains optimal solutions to subproblems
two_conditions.overlapping_subproblems: Same subproblems are solved repeatedly in the naive recursive solution

approaches.memoization: Top-down: solve recursively, cache results in table. Natural recursive structure, lazy evaluation.
approaches.tabulation: Bottom-up: fill table iteratively from smallest subproblems to largest. Usually faster in practice.

steps: Identify the problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Define the state dp[i] or dp[i][j] — what each entry represents
Write the recurrence relation for dp[i] in terms of smaller subproblems
Fill table bottom-up (tabulation) or top-down with memoization
Return dp[n] as the final answer
classic_problems: Rod Cutting — maximize revenue by cutting rod into pieces
Matrix Chain Multiplication — minimize scalar multiplications

example.problem: Rod Cutting — rod of length n, price p[i] for length i. Maximize revenue.
example.recurrence: r(n) = max(p[i] + r[n-i]) for i = 1 to n
example.naive: Exponential O(2ⁿ) — exponentially many ways to cut

complexity.time: O(n²) for most problems, O(nW) for knapsack
complexity.space: O(n) to O(n²) depending on problem

comparisons.greedy: DP considers all subproblem solutions and picks optimal. Greedy makes one locally optimal choice — faster but not always correct.
comparisons.divide_conquer: Divide and Conquer: independent subproblems (no overlap). DP: overlapping subproblems stored in table.

name: Greedy Algorithms
definition: A greedy algorithm makes the locally optimal choice at each step, hoping to find a global optimum. Unlike DP, it never reconsiders choices. Works when the problem has the greedy choice property.
intuition: Always pick what looks best right now. Like making change with coins — always use the largest denomination coin that fits.
greedy_choice_property: A globally optimal solution can be arrived at by making a locally optimal (greedy) choice.
steps: Prove the problem has optimal substructure
Prove the greedy choice property holds
Design greedy rule (what to pick at each step)
Apply greedy choice, reduce to smaller subproblem
classic_problems: Activity Selection — select maximum non-overlapping activities
Huffman Coding — optimal prefix-free binary codes

example.problem: Activity Selection: given activities with start/finish times, select maximum compatible set
example.greedy_rule: Always select the activity with the earliest finish time
example.intuition: Finishing early leaves maximum room for future activities
example.proof: Exchange argument: any optimal solution can be converted to include earliest-finish activity without losing optimality

complexity.time: O(n log n) for activity selection (sorting dominates)

comparisons.dynamic_programming: Greedy is faster but only correct when greedy choice property holds. DP always finds optimal but slower.
comparisons.brute_force: Greedy is polynomial; brute force exponential.

name: BFS (Breadth-First Search)
definition: BFS explores a graph level by level, visiting all vertices at distance k from source before any vertex at distance k+1. Uses a FIFO queue. Computes shortest paths in unweighted graphs.
intuition: Like ripples in a pond — explore all vertices one hop away, then two hops, then three. Use a queue to process in order.
steps: Initialize: color all vertices WHITE, distance = ∞, predecessor = NIL
While Q is not empty: dequeue vertex u
For each adjacent vertex v: if v is WHITE, set v.color=GRAY, v.d=u.d+1, v.π=u, enqueue v
pseudocode:
BFS(G, s):
  for each u in G.V - {s}:
    u.color = WHITE, u.d = ∞, u.π = NIL
  s.color = GRAY, s.d = 0, s.π = NIL
  Q = empty queue
  ENQUEUE(Q, s)
  while Q ≠ empty:
    u = DEQUEUE(Q)
    for each v in G.Adj[u]:
      if v.color == WHITE:
        v.color = GRAY
        v.d = u.d + 1
        v.π = u
        ENQUEUE(Q, v)
    u.color = BLACK

steps: Process s: neighbors r,w → r.d=1, w.d=1
Process w: neighbors s(v),t,x → t.d=2, x.d=2
example.output: Shortest distances from s to all reachable vertices

comparisons.dfs: BFS finds shortest paths (unweighted), DFS doesn't. BFS uses queue, DFS uses stack. DFS better for topological sort, cycle detection.
comparisons.dijkstra: BFS = shortest path for unweighted graphs. Dijkstra = shortest path for weighted graphs.

name: DFS (Depth-First Search)
definition: DFS explores a graph by going as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. It timestamps each vertex with discovery time d[v] and finish time f[v], useful for topological sort and SCC.
intuition: Like exploring a maze — go as far as possible, when stuck backtrack, try another path. Use a stack (or recursion).
steps: Initialize all vertices WHITE, predecessor NIL, time = 0
For each unvisited vertex u: call DFS-VISIT(u)
DFS-VISIT(u): color u GRAY, record discovery time d[u]
For each neighbor v of u: if v is WHITE, set v.π = u, recurse DFS-VISIT(v)
Color u BLACK, record finish time f[u]
pseudocode:
DFS(G):
  for each u in G.V: u.color=WHITE, u.π=NIL
  time = 0
  for each u in G.V:
    if u.color == WHITE: DFS-VISIT(G, u)

DFS-VISIT(G, u):
  time = time + 1, u.d = time
  u.color = GRAY
  for each v in G.Adj[u]:
    if v.color == WHITE:
      v.π = u
      DFS-VISIT(G, v)
  u.color = BLACK
  time = time + 1, u.f = time

example.output: Discovery/finish times reveal tree edges, back edges (cycles), forward edges, cross edges

edge_classification.tree_edge: Edge in DFS tree — v discovered from u
edge_classification.back_edge: Edge u→v where v is ancestor of u — indicates cycle
edge_classification.forward_edge: Edge u→v where v is descendant of u (DAGs only)

comparisons.bfs: DFS uses O(V) stack space, BFS uses O(V) queue. DFS better for topological sort; BFS better for shortest paths.

name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
definition: Dijkstra's Algorithm solves the single-source shortest paths problem on a weighted directed graph with non-negative edge weights. It greedily selects the nearest unvisited vertex and relaxes its edges.
intuition: Always expand the unvisited vertex with the smallest known distance. Like GPS — always take the road that gets you to your destination most efficiently.
steps: Initialize: d[s]=0, d[v]=∞ for all v≠s, π[v]=NIL
Add all vertices to min-priority queue Q
While Q is not empty: extract vertex u with minimum d[u]
Add u to set S (permanently settled)
For each neighbor v of u: RELAX(u, v, w)
RELAX: if d[u] + w(u,v) < d[v]: update d[v] = d[u] + w(u,v), π[v] = u
Extract y(5): relax t→8, x→14, z→7
Extract z(7): relax x→13, s stays

correctness: Proved by loop invariant: at start of each iteration, d[v] = δ(s,v) for all v in S
limitations: Does NOT work with negative edge weights

comparisons.bellman_ford: Dijkstra: O((V+E)logV), no negative edges. Bellman-Ford: O(VE), handles negative edges.
comparisons.bfs: BFS: shortest path in unweighted graphs. Dijkstra: shortest path in weighted graphs with non-negative weights.
comparisons.floyd_warshall: Dijkstra: single-source O((V+E)logV). Floyd-Warshall: all-pairs O(V³).

name: Divide and Conquer
definition: Divide and Conquer is an algorithm design paradigm that breaks a problem into smaller independent subproblems, solves them recursively, and combines their solutions. Running time expressed as recurrence T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n).
intuition: Split a big problem into smaller problems of the same type. Solve each small problem. Combine the small solutions.

three_steps.divide: Break the problem into smaller subproblems of the same type
three_steps.conquer: Solve subproblems recursively (base case: small enough to solve directly)
three_steps.combine: Merge subproblem solutions into the solution for the original problem

examples: Merge Sort: divide array, sort halves, merge
Binary Search: check middle, recurse on half
steps: Define how to divide into subproblems

complexity.time: Depends on a, b, f(n) — use Master Theorem
complexity.space: O(log n) to O(n) recursion stack

comparisons.dynamic_programming: D&C: independent subproblems. DP: overlapping subproblems, store results.
comparisons.greedy: D&C solves subproblems optimally. Greedy makes single choices without looking back.

name: Bubble Sort
definition: Bubble Sort repeatedly passes through the array, comparing adjacent elements and swapping them if out of order. After each pass, the largest unsorted element 'bubbles' to its correct position.
intuition: Like bubbles rising in water — each pass makes the largest remaining element float to the top.
steps: Outer loop: i from 0 to n-2
Inner loop: j from 0 to n-2-i
After each outer iteration, A[n-1-i] is in its final position
Optimization: if no swap in a pass, array is sorted (break early)
pseudocode:
BUBBLE-SORT(A):
  for i = 0 to A.length - 2
    for j = 0 to A.length - 2 - i
      if A[j] > A[j+1]
        swap A[j] with A[j+1]

time.best: O(n) — with early termination on sorted array

disadvantages: O(n²) average and worst — very slow for large n

name: Selection Sort
definition: Selection Sort repeatedly finds the minimum element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning. It makes exactly n-1 swaps.
steps: Find the minimum element in A[i..n-1]
pseudocode:
SELECTION-SORT(A):
  for i = 0 to A.length - 2
    min_idx = i
    for j = i+1 to A.length - 1
      if A[j] < A[min_idx]: min_idx = j
    swap A[i] with A[min_idx]
advantages: Exactly n-1 swaps (good when writes are costly)
disadvantages: Always O(n²) — even on sorted array

name: Lower Bounds for Sorting
definition: Any comparison-based sorting algorithm must make Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case. This is proven using decision trees — a model that captures all possible comparison sequences.

proof.model: Decision tree: binary tree where each internal node represents a comparison A[i] vs A[j], leaves are permutations
proof.leaves: Must have at least n! leaves (one per permutation of n elements)
proof.height: A binary tree with n! leaves has height ≥ log₂(n!) = Ω(n log n)
proof.conclusion: Worst case comparisons ≥ Ω(n log n)

why_counting_sort_beats_it: Counting Sort, Radix Sort, and Bucket Sort are NOT comparison-based — they use integer properties to sort faster.

name: Minimum Spanning Tree
definition: A Minimum Spanning Tree of a connected weighted undirected graph is a spanning tree whose total edge weight is minimized. Kruskal's and Prim's algorithms both find MST greedily.

kruskal.approach: Edge-based greedy. Sort all edges by weight. Add edge if it doesn't create a cycle (use Union-Find).
kruskal.complexity: O(E log E) = O(E log V)

prim.approach: Vertex-based greedy. Grow MST one vertex at a time. Always add cheapest edge connecting tree to non-tree vertex.
prim.complexity: O(E log V) with binary heap
prim.pseudocode:
Initialize key[s]=0, key[v]=∞ for others
While not all vertices in MST:
  u = vertex with minimum key not in MST
  For each neighbor v: if w(u,v) < key[v]: update key[v]

cut_property: For any cut (S, V-S), the minimum weight edge crossing the cut belongs to some MST
